children.name: Atrichornis
children.description: A genus of two elusive, ground-dwelling passerines endemic to Australia, known for their extraordinarily loud voices relative to their small size. They inhabit dense, moist undergrowth in forests and heathlands, where they are more often heard than seen. Scrubbirds have a peculiar, rounded tail and strong legs adapted for a terrestrial lifestyle.

children.name: Atrichornis rufescens
children.description: A secretive, rufous-brown bird with a loud, penetrating song that belies its small size, found in the rainforests of northern New South Wales and southeastern Queensland. It forages in leaf litter for insects and other invertebrates, rarely taking flight. Habitat fragmentation and altered fire regimes threaten this already restricted-range species.

children.name: Atrichornis clamosus
children.description: One of the rarest and most celebrated Australian birds, the Noisy Scrubbird was thought extinct for over 50 years before being rediscovered in 1961 at Two Peoples Bay, Western Australia. It produces one of the loudest songs of any bird its size, a piercing series of whistles and rattles audible from over a kilometre away. Intensive conservation management has slowly increased its population from a handful of individuals to several hundred.
